rationale: Title X substantially outperforms Y on the most important criteria for a technical HR paper. It specifies the discriminative marker, the clinical application (PVI confirmation), the validated cutoff (300 Hz), the sample size (30 patients), and the discriminative performance (AUC 0.99) — giving readers and reviewers an immediate, complete picture of the study's contribution. Title Y is cleaner and more readable but is largely a topic label: it names the marker and dual function but omits all quantitative specifics, leaving the reader without any sense of the finding's magnitude or clinical precision. In EP, where exact thresholds and performance metrics drive adoption, X's informational density is a decisive advantage.
loser_weakness: Title Y reads as a descriptive framework title rather than a results-driven title. The phrase 'quantitative marker' promises numerics but delivers none; without the 300-Hz threshold or AUC the reader cannot assess novelty or clinical utility from the title alone, reducing both appeal and informativeness.
